narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background, your law school application stood out in some ways but faced challenges in key academic areas that the admissions model weighs heavily. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 sits a bit below the typical 3.21 average, while your Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles both at 4 are lower than the usual 5.71 and 5.53, reflecting performance in the middle-to-lower range compared to peers. Your LSAT score of 22.50 is notably below the dataset average of 36.70, and you're planning to attend full-time, which is the norm for over 92% of students. The model predicts a 58.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, tipping the scales just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection—essentially, it flagged you as higher risk for not passing compared to applicants likely to succeed. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your LSAT score, as its lower-than-average level strongly signaled to the model a reduced likelihood of bar exam success for someone in your position. Next in importance, your undergraduate GPA worked in your favor, pulling the prediction somewhat toward passing by showing decent prior academic capability, even if below average. Your middle family income bracket also helped mildly, nudging things positively since it aligns with many successful students' backgrounds. Overall, while your GPA and income provided some counterbalance, the LSAT's dominant negative pull outweighed them, making your profile riskier than the typical admitted student's stronger test scores and balanced metrics. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-class family—many in similar demographics succeed with targeted improvements. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above 36.70, as that single change could dramatically shift future predictions. Strengthening your overall academic narrative through additional coursework or explanations in reapplications might also help highlight your resilience. With focused effort, you can reapply stronger and turn this around.
